title: Marie Curie
teaser: Meet the scientist from Poland who moved to Paris with almost nothing, discovered two new elements, coined the word 'radioactivity,' and became the first person in history to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences.

summary.intro: Marie Curie (1867–1934) was a physicist and chemist from Warsaw, Poland, who became one of the greatest scientists who ever lived. At a time when women were not allowed to attend university in her homeland, she secretly studied, then moved to Paris to earn degrees in both physics and mathematics. In a leaky shed, working beside her husband Pierre, she discovered two new chemical elements and changed how the world understood atoms. She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize — and then she went even further, winning a second Nobel Prize in an entirely different science. No one has ever matched that achievement.

sections.title: A Girl Who Loved to Learn
sections.body: Marie Curie was born Maria Sklodowska on November 7, 1867, in Warsaw, Poland — a country then controlled by Russia. Her father was a physics and mathematics teacher who let her use his scientific instruments at home, sparking a lifelong love of science. Tragedy came early: her mother died of tuberculosis when Maria was just ten years old. Under Russian rule, girls were forbidden from attending university, so Maria secretly attended the 'Flying University' — an underground school that moved from home to home to avoid being shut down by Russian authorities. Determined to get a real education, she and her sister Bronya made a daring pact: Maria would work as a governess for years, sending money to Paris so Bronya could study medicine. Once Bronya became a doctor, she would support Maria's own studies. It was a plan that required extraordinary patience and sacrifice — but Maria kept her word, and Bronya kept hers.

sections.title: From Warsaw to Paris
sections.body: In 1891, at the age of 23, Maria finally boarded a train for Paris to begin the education she had waited so long for. She enrolled at the Sorbonne — the University of Paris — and threw herself into her studies, living in a tiny, freezing attic apartment and sometimes surviving on little more than bread and chocolate. In 1893, she earned her degree in physical sciences, becoming the first woman ever to earn a physics degree from the Sorbonne. She followed it with a mathematics degree in 1894, finishing at the top of her class. That same year, a mutual friend introduced her to a French physicist named Pierre Curie, who had already made important discoveries in magnetism. Their shared passion for science quickly grew into something more. They married on July 26, 1895, and from that day on they were partners in both life and laboratory — a team that would change the world.

sections.title: Discovering Radioactivity
sections.body: In 1896, a French scientist named Henri Becquerel discovered that uranium gave off mysterious invisible rays. Marie decided to study the phenomenon for her doctoral research — a subject almost no one else was interested in. She invented instruments to measure the rays precisely, and she coined a brand-new word for what she observed: 'radioactivity.' As she dug deeper, she realized that pitchblende — a heavy black uranium ore — gave off even more radiation than pure uranium alone. Something unknown was hiding inside it. Pierre set aside his own research to join her, and together they built a makeshift laboratory in a leaky shed. They processed ton after ton of pitchblende, boiling it, filtering it, and refining it with their bare hands. In July 1898, they announced the discovery of polonium — named after Marie's beloved homeland. In December 1898, they announced a second discovery: radium. Two new elements, found through four years of almost unimaginable effort.

sections.title: Two Nobel Prizes
sections.body: In 1903, the Nobel Committee awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics to Henri Becquerel, Pierre Curie, and Marie Curie for their work on radioactivity — making Marie the first woman in history to win a Nobel Prize. Then, on April 19, 1906, tragedy struck. Pierre was crossing a Paris street when he slipped and was killed by a horse-drawn wagon. Marie was devastated. But rather than give up, she took over Pierre's professorship at the Sorbonne, becoming the first woman ever to hold a professorship there. She continued their research alone, finding ways to isolate pure radium and measure it precisely. In 1911, the Nobel Committee awarded her a second prize — the Nobel Prize in Chemistry — for the discovery of polonium and radium. Marie Curie became the first and only person in history to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. She accepted the prize alone, still grieving, still working.

sections.title: A Legacy That Changed the World
sections.body: Marie Curie never stopped working, even as the radiation she handled daily was slowly damaging her body. When World War I broke out in 1914, she fitted vehicles with X-ray equipment and drove them to battlefield hospitals herself — earning the nickname 'petites Curies' for her mobile X-ray units. She trained over 150 women as X-ray technicians and helped treat more than a million wounded soldiers. She founded the Curie Institute in Paris (1920) and in Warsaw (1932), both of which still research cancer treatments today. Her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie followed in her footsteps and won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1935. Marie herself died on July 4, 1934, from aplastic anemia caused by decades of radiation exposure. In 1995, France honored her by moving her remains to the Panthéon — the great monument that holds France's most extraordinary citizens — making her the first woman enshrined there on her own merits.

keyFacts: Marie Curie was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the only person ever to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences.
She coined the word 'radioactivity' and discovered two new elements: polonium (named after Poland) and radium, both in 1898.
Marie and Pierre worked in a leaky shed — often called 'a cross between a stable and a potato cellar' — to isolate their discoveries.
During World War I, Marie built mobile X-ray units and drove them to the front lines herself, helping treat over a million wounded soldiers.
Her laboratory notebooks are still so radioactive today that researchers must wear protective gear and sign a safety waiver just to read them.

videos.title: Biography of Marie Curie for Kids: Famous Scientists for Children - FreeSchool
videos.description: FreeSchool's narrated biography covers Marie Curie's life from her childhood in Poland through her discovery of radioactivity and her two Nobel Prizes — a thorough, calm introduction well-suited as a first overview of the topic.

videos.title: MARIE CURIE | The Woman Who Saved HUNDREDS OF LIVES | Educational Videos | Happy Learning
videos.description: Happy Learning's animated video focuses on how Marie Curie's discoveries — especially portable X-ray units in World War I — saved countless lives, making it a great companion to the Legacy chapter.

videos.title: Who Was Marie Curie? - Facts for Kids
videos.description: A concise facts-for-kids overview of Marie Curie's most important achievements — her discoveries, her Nobel Prizes, and why she remains an inspiration for young scientists today.

readings.title: I Am Marie Curie
readings.author: Brad Meltzer
readings.citationChicago: Meltzer, Brad. 2020. I Am Marie Curie. Illustrated by Christopher Eliopoulos. New York: Dial Books for Young Readers.
readings.summary: Part of Brad Meltzer's beloved Ordinary People Change the World series, this picture book tells Marie Curie's story in a warm, conversational style — as if Marie herself is speaking directly to the reader. Meltzer focuses on the qualities that made her extraordinary: her refusal to give up when doors were slammed in her face, her insatiable curiosity, and her belief that science belonged to everyone regardless of gender. The book follows her from a girl secretly studying in Warsaw through her Nobel Prize victories, with illustrator Christopher Eliopoulos bringing the story to life in a colorful comic-book style. It ends with the reminder that Marie's greatest discovery was the courage to keep asking questions no matter what anyone told her.

readings.title: Who Was Marie Curie?
readings.author: Megan Stine
readings.citationChicago: Stine, Megan. 2014. Who Was Marie Curie? Illustrated by Marta Antelo. New York: Grosset & Dunlap.
readings.summary: Part of the hugely popular Who Was? chapter-book series, this biography is written for readers aged 8–12 and tells Marie Curie's full story in accessible, engaging prose. Megan Stine covers Marie's difficult childhood in Russian-controlled Poland, her years of self-sacrifice to study in Paris, her groundbreaking research with Pierre, and both Nobel Prize wins. The book uses black-and-white illustrations and a friendly tone to make science history feel like an adventure story. It also places Marie's work in historical context — explaining why it was so astonishing for a woman to achieve what she achieved in the 1890s and 1900s — without ever becoming preachy or dry. A perfect step-up read for any child who has finished the picture books and wants to go deeper.

readings.title: Marie Curie — Biographical (Nobel Prize Official Page)
readings.author: Nobel Prize Outreach
readings.citationChicago: Nobel Prize Outreach. "Marie Curie — Biographical." NobelPrize.org. https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/physics/1903/marie-curie/biographical/.
readings.summary: The official Nobel Prize website's biographical page on Marie Curie is one of the most authoritative and accessible primary-source references available online. Written at a level older children and parents can read together, it covers her early life in Warsaw, her studies at the Sorbonne, her research on radioactivity, and both Nobel Prize awards in the words of the Nobel Committee itself. The page includes historical photographs, links to her Nobel lectures, and a full list of her scientific publications — making it an outstanding reference for students doing reports or anyone who wants to go beyond the children's books. Best for ages 10 and up as an independent read, or for younger children with a parent.

learnMoreImages.caption: Marie Skłodowska photographed at age 16 in Warsaw, 1883 — sixteen years after she was born in this Russian-controlled city on November 7, 1867. Growing up here, she was denied access to a university education, but her father's love of science ignited a curiosity that could not be contained.

The photo collection on display at the Maria Skłodowska-Curie Museum in Warsaw, housed in the tenement at 16 Freta Street where she was born. Under Russian occupation, Marie secretly attended the 'Flying University' — an underground network of classes that moved from home to home to avoid being shut down by authorities who forbade women from studying.

Marie Curie photographed for her Nobel Prize circa 1903 — the ultimate reward for a plan she and her sister Bronya hatched years earlier in Warsaw. Marie worked as a governess, sending money to Bronya studying medicine in Paris; once Bronya became a doctor, she funded Marie's own studies. That selfless pact made this Nobel portrait possible.

Marie Curie photographed circa 1898, on the eve of her greatest discoveries. Her father Władysław Skłodowski was a physics and mathematics teacher in Warsaw who let her handle his scientific instruments as a child — giving her an early, hands-on feel for measurement and precision that would define her entire scientific career.

Marie Curie in her Paris laboratory in 1912, by then one of the world's most celebrated scientists. She was born Maria Sklodowska in Warsaw in 1867 and always signed her name 'Marie Skłodowska-Curie' to honor her Polish roots — the country she named polonium after would never leave her.

questions.prompt: In what city was Marie Curie born?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie was born Maria Sklodowska on November 7, 1867, in Warsaw, Poland — a country that was under Russian rule at the time, which greatly shaped her difficult early years.

questions.prompt: Why was Marie unable to attend university in Poland as a young woman?
choices: There were no universities in Poland at the time
Her family could not afford any school fees at all
Girls were forbidden from attending university under Russian rule
Her father refused to allow women in the family to study
questions.correctAnswer: Girls were forbidden from attending university under Russian rule
questions.explanation: Poland was controlled by Russia, and Russian authorities did not allow women to attend the official universities. Marie secretly attended the 'Flying University' — an underground school that moved from home to home to avoid being shut down.

questions.prompt: What arrangement did Marie make with her sister Bronya to help them both get an education?
choices: They agreed to study the same subject so they could share one set of books
Marie would work and pay for Bronya's studies first, then Bronya would fund Marie's
They planned to teach each other at home instead of going abroad
Bronya would find work in Poland while Marie studied in Germany
questions.correctAnswer: Marie would work and pay for Bronya's studies first, then Bronya would fund Marie's
questions.explanation: The two sisters made a selfless pact: Marie would work as a governess to send money to Bronya studying medicine in Paris, and once Bronya became a doctor she would support Marie's own studies. It was a plan that required years of patience and sacrifice.

questions.prompt: Marie Curie's father was a teacher of physics and mathematics.
questions.explanation: True. Władysław Skłodowski was a teacher of physics and mathematics in Warsaw. He encouraged Marie's love of science from a very young age, letting her use his scientific instruments and teaching her to think carefully about how the world worked.

questions.prompt: What was Marie Curie's original birth name before she moved to France?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie was born Maria Sklodowska on November 7, 1867, in Warsaw, Poland. She later Frenchified her first name to Marie after settling in Paris, and took the surname Curie when she married Pierre Curie in 1895.

aspects.name: Moving to Paris and the Sorbonne

learnMoreImages.caption: The Galerie Richelieu, the historic interior gallery of the Sorbonne in Paris. In 1891, at the age of 23, Marie arrived in Paris with little money and enrolled at this ancient university — the first step in the education she had been dreaming of and sacrificing for since her teenage years in Warsaw.

Marie Curie photographed circa 1900, the same intense focus visible that carried her through years of grueling study at the Sorbonne. She lived in a tiny attic apartment near the university, sometimes forgot to eat, and studied by lamplight late into the night — but she finished near the top of her class.

Commemorative plaque at 10 rue Vauquelin, Paris — marking the laboratory where Marie Curie made her greatest discoveries. It was the same city where she had earned her physics degree in 1893, becoming the first woman to do so at the Sorbonne — the beginning of a record-breaking scientific career.

Marie Curie measuring radioactivity in her Paris laboratory, circa 1900 — using the precise electrical techniques she had mastered at the Sorbonne, where she earned both a physics degree in 1893 and a mathematics degree in 1894, finishing near the top of her class in both fields.

Pierre Curie photographed by Dujardin, circa 1906 (published in Marie Curie's 1910 Treatise on Radioactivity). Marie met Pierre in 1894 through a mutual friend who thought Pierre might share laboratory space with her. Their shared passion for science quickly became love, and they married on July 26, 1895.

questions.prompt: In what year did Marie move to Paris to begin her university studies?
questions.explanation: Marie moved to Paris in 1891, at the age of 23, finally able to pursue the university education that had been denied to her in Russian-controlled Poland. She enrolled at the Sorbonne and threw herself into her studies.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the famous Paris university where Marie Curie studied?
questions.explanation: Marie studied at the University of Paris, known as the Sorbonne — one of the oldest and most respected universities in the world. She lived in a tiny, freezing attic apartment nearby and studied so hard she sometimes forgot to eat.

questions.prompt: What historic achievement did Marie accomplish at the Sorbonne that no woman had done before?
choices: She became the first female professor of science
She earned a physics degree — the first woman to do so at the Sorbonne
She won the university's top prize for mathematics
She set up the first women-only science laboratory there
questions.correctAnswer: She earned a physics degree — the first woman to do so at the Sorbonne
questions.explanation: In 1893, Marie Curie earned her degree in physical sciences from the Sorbonne, becoming the first woman to earn a physics degree there. She followed it with a mathematics degree in 1894, finishing near the top of her class.

questions.prompt: Marie Curie earned degrees in both physics and mathematics from the Sorbonne.
questions.explanation: True. Marie earned a degree in physical sciences in 1893 and a degree in mathematics in 1894 — both from the Sorbonne. She was an extraordinary student who finished near the top of her class in both fields.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the scientist Marie met in Paris who became her husband and research partner?
questions.explanation: Marie met Pierre Curie in 1894 through a mutual friend who hoped he might share his laboratory space with her. Their shared passion for science quickly grew into love, and they married on July 26, 1895. Together they would change the course of science forever.

learnMoreImages.caption: Marie Curie photographed circa 1898 — the year she coined the word 'radioactivity' to describe the invisible energy given off by certain elements. Her careful measurements proved this energy came from inside individual atoms, not from any chemical reaction, upending everything scientists thought they knew about matter.

Marie Skłodowska at age 16 in Warsaw, 1883 — years before she would discover a new element and name it polonium after this very city and country. Poland had been erased from the map of Europe, divided between Russia, Prussia, and Austria. Naming her element after it was an act of love and defiance.

Uraninite (pitchblende) from a German uranium deposit — the same type of heavy black ore that Marie and Pierre Curie processed by the ton in their leaky Paris shed. From mountains of pitchblende they extracted only tiny traces of radium, a labor that took four years and damaged their health but yielded a discovery that would one day help treat cancer.

Pierre and Marie Curie in their Paris laboratory, circa 1904, with Marie operating the precise ionization apparatus used to detect radioactivity. The discoveries of polonium and radium were a true partnership: when Marie identified that pitchblende contained unknown radioactive elements, Pierre set aside his own magnetism research entirely to join her.

Pierre and Marie Curie in their Paris laboratory, 1900 — just two years after their historic announcement of radium in December 1898. That year the Curies discovered two brand-new elements, polonium and radium, representing two of the most significant additions to the periodic table in the entire 19th century.

questions.prompt: What important scientific word did Marie Curie invent to describe the energy given off by certain elements?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie coined the term 'radioactivity' to describe the invisible energy that certain elements — like uranium — continuously give off. Her careful research showed that this energy came from inside the atom itself, not from a chemical reaction.

questions.prompt: Which new element did Marie name after her home country, Poland?
questions.explanation: Marie named the element polonium after Poland, her beloved homeland — which at the time was not even an independent country. Discovering polonium in 1898 was her way of putting Poland on the map of the world, even when its rulers tried to erase it.

questions.prompt: What heavy mineral ore did Marie and Pierre process by the ton to isolate tiny amounts of radium?
questions.explanation: Marie and Pierre processed enormous quantities of pitchblende (a uranium-rich ore) in a leaky, drafty shed to isolate radium. It took four years of exhausting work and tons of ore to produce just one gram of radium — the element that would one day help treat cancer.

questions.prompt: Marie Curie discovered polonium and radium entirely on her own, without any collaboration from Pierre.
questions.explanation: False. Marie and Pierre Curie were true partners in science. After Marie identified that pitchblende contained unknown radioactive elements, Pierre set aside his own research to join her. They made the discoveries of polonium and radium together in 1898 as a team.

questions.prompt: In what year did Marie and Pierre Curie announce the discovery of radium?
questions.explanation: Marie and Pierre Curie announced their discovery of radium in December 1898. Along with polonium (also announced that year), it represented one of the greatest scientific breakthroughs of the 19th century — two brand-new elements found through years of painstaking work.

learnMoreImages.caption: Henri Becquerel photographed by Paul Nadar — the French physicist who shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Marie and Pierre Curie. Becquerel had first discovered uranium's mysterious rays in 1896, sparking Marie's research into radioactivity and setting her on the path to two Nobel Prizes.

Marie Curie photographed for her Nobel Prize circa 1903 — the first of two Nobel Prize portraits she would sit for. No one in history has ever matched her record: Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Marie Curie circa 1900 — inspired by Becquerel's 1896 discovery that uranium gave off mysterious rays, she was already deep in the research that would transform science. She coined the word 'radioactivity,' traced its source to the atom itself, and discovered that pitchblende hid two previously unknown elements.

Pierre Curie photographed by Dujardin, circa 1906 — the year of his tragic death in a Paris street accident on April 19. Marie accepted the 1911 Nobel Prize in Chemistry alone, five years after losing her husband and scientific partner. That she continued their work at all, let alone won a second Nobel, is a testament to extraordinary resilience.

The commemorative plaque at 10 rue Vauquelin, Paris — marking the laboratory where Marie and Pierre discovered polonium and radium in 1898. In 1911, the Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Marie alone in recognition of those discoveries, cementing a site once described as 'a cross between a stable and a potato cellar' as one of the most important addresses in the history of science.

questions.prompt: In what field of science was Marie Curie's first Nobel Prize awarded?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie's first Nobel Prize was in Physics, awarded in 1903. She shared it with her husband Pierre Curie and scientist Henri Becquerel for their research into the spontaneous radiation that Becquerel had first discovered in 1896.

questions.prompt: What made Marie Curie's Nobel Prize record completely unique in the history of science?
choices: She was the first scientist of any kind to win a Nobel Prize
She won a Nobel Prize three times — more than anyone else
She won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences — the only person ever to do so
She won her first Nobel Prize at age 16 — the youngest winner ever
questions.correctAnswer: She won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences — the only person ever to do so
questions.explanation: Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 and the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911 — making her the first and only person in history to win Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields. No one has ever matched this achievement.

questions.prompt: Which scientist shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Marie and Pierre Curie?
questions.explanation: Henri Becquerel shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Marie and Pierre Curie. Becquerel had first discovered that uranium gave off mysterious rays in 1896, which inspired Marie to investigate the phenomenon — eventually leading her to the word 'radioactivity.'

questions.prompt: Marie Curie's second Nobel Prize, in Chemistry in 1911, was awarded to her alone.
questions.explanation: True. The 1911 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Marie Curie alone, in recognition of her discovery of polonium and radium and her work isolating radium. Pierre had died in a tragic street accident in 1906, so Marie carried on her work — and accepted this second Nobel Prize — by herself.

questions.prompt: In what year did Marie Curie receive her second Nobel Prize, this time in Chemistry?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911, becoming the first person ever to win two Nobel Prizes. The prize recognized her discovery of polonium and radium and her success in isolating pure radium — a monumental achievement in chemistry.

learnMoreImages.caption: Marie Curie in her Paris laboratory in 1912, still handling radioactive materials without protective equipment — unaware that the invisible radiation she measured every day was slowly damaging her bone marrow. She died on July 4, 1934, from aplastic anemia caused by decades of radiation exposure. Her dedication to science ultimately cost her life.

Marie Curie beside a mobile military hospital X-ray unit, circa 1915 — one of the 'petites Curies' she designed and drove to the front lines of World War I. By fitting vehicles with X-ray equipment powered by a small dynamo, she brought diagnostic radiology directly to battlefield surgeons, helping locate bullets and broken bones in over a million wounded soldiers.

Pierre and Marie Curie in their Paris laboratory, circa 1904. In 1944, scientists at the University of Chicago named element 96 'Curium' in honor of both Marie and Pierre Curie, recognizing that their joint work on radioactivity had opened the entire field of nuclear science that made the discovery of new elements possible.

Marie Curie and her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie at Brazil's National Museum in Rio de Janeiro, August 2, 1926. Nine years after this photograph was taken, Irène won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1935 for discovering artificial radioactivity — just one year after Marie's death. The Curies became the most Nobel Prize–winning family in history.

The tomb of Pierre and Marie Curie in the Panthéon, Paris — France's monument to its greatest citizens. In 1995, Marie's remains were transferred here, making her the first woman enshrined in the Panthéon on her own merits. The girl who was forbidden from attending university in Russian-controlled Poland now rests among France's immortals.

questions.prompt: What illness caused Marie Curie's death in 1934, most likely from years of radiation exposure?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie died on July 4, 1934, from aplastic anemia — a condition where the bone marrow stops making enough blood cells. Scientists believe it was caused by decades of exposure to radiation, which she handled without protective equipment because its dangers were not yet understood.

questions.prompt: What did Marie Curie develop during World War I to help doctors treat wounded soldiers on the front lines?
choices: Mobile X-ray units that could be driven to battlefield hospitals
Protective uniforms to shield nurses from disease
A new surgical technique for removing shrapnel
questions.correctAnswer: Mobile X-ray units that could be driven to battlefield hospitals
questions.explanation: During World War I, Marie Curie fitted vehicles with X-ray equipment and drove them to the front lines so surgeons could see inside soldiers' bodies to locate bullets and broken bones. These vehicles were nicknamed 'petites Curies' — little Curies — and helped save countless lives.

questions.prompt: What element in the periodic table is named in honor of Marie Curie?
questions.explanation: Element 96 in the periodic table is named Curium (symbol Cm) in honor of both Marie and Pierre Curie. It was first created in 1944 by a team of scientists in Chicago and named as a tribute to the Curies' extraordinary contributions to radioactivity research.

questions.prompt: Marie Curie's daughter Irène also won a Nobel Prize in science.
questions.explanation: True. Irène Joliot-Curie, Marie's elder daughter, won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1935 — just one year after her mother's death — for discovering artificial radioactivity. The Curies became the most Nobel Prize-winning family in history, with prizes spanning two generations.

questions.prompt: What famous building in Paris holds Marie Curie's remains, honoring her as one of France's greatest citizens?
questions.explanation: In 1995, Marie Curie's remains were moved to the Panthéon in Paris — the great monument where France honors its most extraordinary citizens. She was the first woman to be enshrined there on her own merits, taking her place alongside writers, philosophers, and national heroes.
